items.want: More consistency on the greens -- fewer three-putts, tighter dispersion, predictable results round after round
items.copy_implication: Lead with the consistency promise. 'From 34-38 putts per round to 28-31 -- consistently.' Use before/after putt-count data as the primary proof metric. The word 'consistent' appears more than any other desire word in the corpus.

items.want: More confidence standing over the ball -- especially on short putts inside 6 feet and under pressure
items.copy_implication: Confidence is the emotional currency of putting. Position RS1 as a 'confidence builder' -- not through mental tricks, but through physics. 'Confidence you can feel before you stroke the ball, because you know the face is already square.'

items.want: More strokes gained -- a lower handicap that finally matches their ball-striking ability
items.copy_implication: Quantify the gain. 'You are a 6 who putts like a 12. The RS1 closes that gap by 3-5 strokes.' Use strokes-gained language this WEB already speaks: SG:Putting, putts per round, putts per GIR.

items.want: More enjoyment of the game -- putting that feels effortless instead of stressful
items.copy_implication: The emotional endgame is not just better scores -- it is enjoying the game again. 'Remember when putting was fun? When you looked forward to getting on the green instead of dreading it?' Appeal to the golfer who has considered quitting because of putting.

items.want: More putts dropping inside 10 feet -- converting birdie opportunities and saving pars
items.copy_implication: The inside-10-feet window is where RS1's face stability has maximum impact. 'Inside 10 feet, face angle determines 80%+ of whether the putt drops. The RS1 keeps the face square through impact. You start making the putts you should be making.'

items.want: Be a complete golfer whose score reflects their actual ability -- not a 'good ball-striker who can't putt'
items.identity_shift: FROM 'I hit the ball great but I can't putt' (split identity, shame) TO 'My putting matches my ball-striking' (unified identity, pride). This is the deepest want in the corpus -- the desire to resolve the identity crisis that defines this WEB.
items.copy_implication: Mirror their exact language: 'You hit 12 greens and shot 82. You know the problem isn't your swing.' Then promise the identity resolution: 'The RS1 makes your scorecard match what everyone at the range already sees.' Never say 'fix your putting' -- say 'make your putting match your game.'

items.want: Be the smart equipment buyer who solved a physics problem -- not the sucker who chases marketing
items.identity_shift: FROM 'I buy whatever has the best marketing' (anti-identity, shame) TO 'I made an engineering-based decision that my playing partners respect' (aspirational identity, pride). This WEB actively fears being seen as gullible.
items.copy_implication: Position the purchase as an intelligent decision, not an emotional one. 'You don't buy drivers without launch monitor data. Why would you buy a putter without understanding the physics?' Frame RS1 as the engineer's choice, not the marketer's pitch.

items.want: Be the golfer at the club everyone asks 'what putter is that?' -- an equipment authority
items.identity_shift: FROM invisible equipment (putter blends in, no one notices) TO conversation-starting equipment (playing partners ask about it, creating social proof and status). The putter becomes a talking point that validates the golfer's intelligence.
items.copy_implication: Social proof through curiosity: 'Someone asks to try my putter almost every round.' Leverage the 'what is that?' moment as a desirable outcome. The RS1 as a quiet status symbol -- not flashy, but visibly different enough to start conversations.

items.want: Be calm under pressure -- the golfer who thrives in tournaments and money games instead of choking
items.identity_shift: FROM 'I fall apart under pressure on the greens' (anxiety, avoidance) TO 'I look forward to pressure putts because I trust my equipment' (confidence, engagement). Competition is where face drift creates maximum damage because grip pressure increases under stress.
items.copy_implication: Connect face stability to pressure performance: 'Under pressure, your grip tightens. With a conventional putter, tighter grip = more face drift. With RS1, forward weighting keeps the face square regardless of grip pressure. The physics don't care about your nerves.'

items.want: Stop three-putting -- eliminate the most visible, scoreboard-destroying failure in their game
items.copy_implication: Three-putting is the single most frequently mentioned pain point (35+ frequency). The achievement is absence: 'Zero three-putts. Not in one round -- in five rounds straight.' Frame it as the first measurable proof that the RS1 is working. Use Q0421: 'Averaging 28 putts per round over the last three rounds with NO 3 PUTTS.'

items.want: Make everything inside 6 feet automatic -- convert short putts from anxiety source to confidence source
items.copy_implication: Short putts are where face angle errors are most punishing and most visible. Position RS1 as the short-putt weapon: 'Inside 6 feet, the RS1 makes it automatic. Not because you changed your stroke. Because the face stays square through impact.' The word 'automatic' appears repeatedly in hope-state quotes.

items.want: Break through their scoring plateau -- shoot in the 70s consistently or reach the next handicap tier
items.copy_implication: Connect putting improvement to the specific milestone they are chasing. 'You know putting is the last variable between you and consistently breaking 80. The RS1 removes that variable.' Use the golfer's own math: 'I'm a 6 right now and I swear I'd be a 2 almost overnight if I just got to be a good putter.'

items.want: End the putter carousel -- find 'the last putter I'll ever buy' and stop searching
items.copy_implication: Carousel fatigue is real and expensive. Position RS1 as the end of the search: 'Every putter you've owned shares the same design flaw: rearward CG that causes face drift. That's why they all failed the same way. The RS1 moves the CG forward. That one change ends the carousel.' Use Q0026: 'This will stay in my bag for a long time.'

items.want: Win bets, club championships, and competitive rounds -- convert better ball-striking into actual competitive results
items.copy_implication: For competitive golfers, putting has financial and social consequences. 'Won all the bets.' The RS1 as competitive advantage: 'Your ball-striking is already good enough to compete. Your putter has been the thing holding you back from winning.'

items.want: Save strokes -- stop wasting 3-7 strokes per round on the putting surface
items.copy_implication: Position wasted strokes as wasted effort: 'You did the hard work hitting 12 greens. Then your putter erased 5 of those opportunities with three-putts.' The RS1 protects the investment of good ball-striking by converting it into actual score.

items.want: Save money -- stop buying new putters every 6-18 months on the carousel
items.copy_implication: The real cost isn't $399 -- it's the $1,000-$3,000 already spent on putters that all failed the same way. 'You've spent more than the RS1 costs on putters you've already discarded. The RS1 is the last one.' Also: RS1 at $399 is $50-150 less than LAB Golf.

items.want: Save mental energy -- stop overthinking every stroke and free the mind for green reading and speed control
items.copy_implication: The mental tax of compensating for face drift is enormous. 'Less to think about' is the desire. Position RS1 as cognitive liberation: 'Forward weighting handles face control so your mind can focus on what actually matters: line and speed. One less variable. One quieter mind.'

items.want: Save practice time -- stop spending hours on the practice green with results that don't transfer to the course
items.copy_implication: The practice-to-course transfer failure is a specific, measurable waste of time. 'You practice 100 putts at home every day. On the practice green, you make everything. On the course, the misses return. The difference? Pressure increases grip pressure, which increases face drift. The RS1 eliminates that variable, so practice gains actually transfer.'

items.want: Avoid the embarrassment of missing short putts in front of playing partners and competitors
items.copy_implication: Missing a 3-footer in front of others is viscerally humiliating. This is the most emotionally charged avoidance want. Don't dwell on the embarrassment in copy -- instead, promise its absence: 'Inside 6 feet, you stop worrying about what your playing partners think. Because the ball goes in.'

items.want: Avoid the putting yips -- the terrifying loss of motor control that ends golf careers
items.copy_implication: The yips are the extreme end of putting anxiety. Many in this WEB are not there yet but fear getting there. RS1 as yips prevention: 'When the putter handles face control mechanically, there's less for your hands to interfere with under pressure. Forward weighting removes the variable that the yips exploit.'

items.want: Avoid wasting $400+ on another putter that doesn't work
items.copy_implication: Investment anxiety is the #1 purchase barrier at this price point. 'What if I spend this and nothing changes?' Address head-on with risk reversal, quantified proof, and the mechanism story: 'Every previous putter failed because of rearward CG. The RS1 moves CG forward. That is not a subjective improvement -- it is a physics change. You will feel it on the first putt.'

items.want: Avoid being 'sold' by marketing hype -- the fear of being a sucker who bought a gimmick
items.copy_implication: This WEB is skeptical by nature. They have been burned by marketing before. Anti-hype positioning is critical: 'We are not going to tell you the RS1 is magic. We are going to show you the physics, give you the data, and let you feel the difference. If that is not enough, it is not the right putter for you.' Undersell, overdeliver.

items.want: Avoid the doom loop -- the self-reinforcing cycle where missed putts destroy confidence, destroyed confidence causes more misses
items.copy_implication: The doom loop is the most insidious pattern in the data: miss --> anxiety --> tighter grip --> more face drift --> miss. RS1 breaks the loop at the physics level: 'Forward weighting keeps the face square regardless of grip pressure. So even when your confidence dips, the putter doesn't drift. The loop breaks because the physics don't care about your mental state.'

emotions.emotion: Frustration -- the maddening disconnect between effort and results on the greens
emotions.trigger_context: Hitting greens in regulation but failing to convert. Watching good approaches wasted by three-putts. Practicing for hours with results that don't transfer. The gap between ball-striking competence and putting incompetence.

emotions.emotion: Anxiety -- physical nervousness and dread standing over short putts, especially under pressure
emotions.trigger_context: Short putts for par or birdie. Tournament and money game situations. The moment of address when hands begin to shake and the mind begins to race. Knowing that the previous miss makes this one harder.

emotions.emotion: Self-doubt -- questioning whether they are fundamentally a bad putter and will always be one
emotions.trigger_context: After multiple failed solutions (new putters, grip changes, lessons, mental techniques). When the doom loop has been running for weeks or months. When practice green success doesn't transfer. 'I can't putt' becomes an identity statement, not a temporary condition.

emotions.emotion: Embarrassment -- the social pain of missing putts that should be routine, in front of others
emotions.trigger_context: Missing a 2-3 footer in competition while others watch. Being asked 'are you in A flight?' after poor putting. The shame of having a scorecard that doesn't reflect visible ball-striking quality.

emotions.emotion: Identity crisis -- the painful split between 'good golfer' (tee to green) and 'bad golfer' (on the green)
emotions.trigger_context: Being complimented on ball-striking then having to confess 'I can't putt.' Knowing that handicap is 3-5 strokes higher than ball-striking ability warrants. Feeling like two different golfers depending on which part of the game is measured.

emotions.emotion: Desperation -- exhausting all conventional solutions and feeling trapped in a problem with no exit
emotions.trigger_context: After trying 5+ putters, multiple grip styles, putting lessons, mental coaching, and practice drills -- nothing sticks. Some golfers reach the point of considering quitting the game entirely. The yips represent the terminal stage of this emotional arc.

emotions.emotion: Anger -- visceral rage at the putter, the game, or themselves after a critical miss
emotions.trigger_context: Missing a putt that costs a match, a personal best, or a meaningful score. The moment after a lip-out or a three-putt from easy range. Often directed at the putter (throwing, slamming) or internalized as self-punishment.

emotions.emotion: Trust -- believing that the putter will do its job without active manipulation
emotions.achievement_context: Standing over a putt and knowing -- not hoping -- that the face is square. Feeling the putter track on-line without steering. The experience of 'letting go' and trusting the equipment. This is the foundational emotion that enables all others.

emotions.emotion: Calm -- quiet mind focused on line and speed instead of mechanical checkpoints
emotions.achievement_context: Putting with only two thoughts: line and speed. No face-angle anxiety. No grip-pressure monitoring. No mechanical compensation. The mental simplicity that comes when the equipment handles the variable you used to fight. 'Less to think about.'

emotions.emotion: Confidence -- expecting putts to drop rather than hoping they will
emotions.achievement_context: Addressing a 4-footer for par with certainty instead of dread. Walking to the green wanting to putt instead of fearing it. The shift from 'will this go in?' to 'I know this is going in if I read it right.' Confidence as the output of consistent face stability, not as a mental trick.

emotions.emotion: Pride -- being recognized as a smart equipment buyer, the golfer others ask for advice
emotions.achievement_context: Playing partners noticing the improvement and asking about the putter. Being able to explain the engineering, not just the brand name. The shift from hiding equipment failures to showcasing equipment intelligence. 'Someone asks to try my putter almost every round.'

emotions.emotion: Joy -- rediscovering the pleasure of putting and the game itself
emotions.achievement_context: Looking forward to putting instead of dreading it. Enjoying practice again. Experiencing 'magical' rounds where everything clicks. The emotional return on investment when a $399 putter transforms the most stressful part of the game into the most enjoyable.

emotions.emotion: Mastery -- the settled feeling of having solved the putting problem permanently
emotions.achievement_context: Averaging 28-31 putts per round consistently. No three-putts for stretches of 5+ rounds. Positive strokes gained putting. The carousel is over. The putter stays in the bag for years. 'This will stay in my bag for a long time.' The problem is solved.

to_disrupt.belief: Putting is mostly mental -- equipment barely matters, it's all about confidence and technique
to_disrupt.counter_evidence: Face angle at impact accounts for 80%+ of directional error on putts. A putter with rearward CG physically drifts open during the stroke, requiring active compensation. Golfers who switch to face-stable putters report measurable improvement (Q0363: '+2.3 strokes gained') even without changing technique. The 'it's all mental' belief prevents golfers from addressing the mechanical root cause.
to_disrupt.disruption_angle: Reframe the relationship: confidence is caused BY equipment performance, not independent of it. When the face stays square mechanically, confidence follows. 'It's not that confidence makes you putt better. It's that a putter that works gives you confidence.' The mental game improves because the physics improved.
to_disrupt.copy_hook: You've been told putting is 'all mental.' Here's what nobody told you: when your putter face drifts open 2.5 degrees at impact -- every single stroke -- no amount of confidence can compensate for bad physics.

to_disrupt.belief: All putters perform essentially the same -- the difference is in the golfer, not the club
to_disrupt.counter_evidence: SAM PuttLab data shows measurable face angle consistency differences between putter designs. Forward-weighted and zero-torque putters keep the face square through a significantly larger impact zone than conventional putters (Q0412: 'DF3 stayed square through a 5 inch zone compared to 2 inches'). Dispersion patterns are objectively tighter. The 'all putters are the same' belief is factually incorrect and measurably disproven.
to_disrupt.disruption_angle: Challenge with data, not argument. 'Don't take our word for it. Measure it. A conventional putter stays square through approximately 2 inches of the impact zone. The RS1 stays square through 5+. That's not confidence. That's physics.'
to_disrupt.copy_hook: Every putter you've ever owned has the same design flaw. The CG sits behind the shaft, so the face drifts open unless you actively hold it square. You've been compensating for bad physics your entire putting career.

to_disrupt.belief: If I just practice more, my putting will improve -- the answer is more reps, not different equipment
to_disrupt.counter_evidence: Multiple golfers report practicing hundreds of putts daily with no improvement on the course (Q0060: '100 3 footers, 100 6 footers, and 100 9 footers every day'). The practice-to-course transfer failure happens because practice conditions have lower pressure (lower grip pressure = less face drift), so the equipment flaw is masked. Practice cannot solve an equipment problem.
to_disrupt.disruption_angle: Validate the effort, redirect the blame. 'You are putting in the work. The problem is not your dedication -- it's that you're practicing with a putter that fights you differently under pressure than it does on the practice green. When grip pressure rises, face drift increases. That's why practice green success doesn't transfer.'
to_disrupt.copy_hook: You practice 100 putts a day. On the practice green, you make everything. On the course, the misses return. It's not your technique that changes -- it's your grip pressure. And your putter punishes you for it.

to_disrupt.belief: My putting problems are caused by my stroke mechanics -- I need lessons, not a new putter
to_disrupt.counter_evidence: Q0505: 'Stroke is very consistent, but consistently leaving putterface 2.5+ degrees open at impact. Tempo good, all other measures look good.' The golfer's stroke can be mechanically sound while the putter face still drifts open due to CG placement. Q0101: 'Felt like I was always fighting the putter. Thought it was my stroke.' Many golfers discover that their stroke was fine -- the putter was the variable.
to_disrupt.disruption_angle: Validate their stroke, blame the putter's physics. 'Your stroke isn't the problem. Your SAM PuttLab data is probably fine. But your putter's CG sits behind the shaft, so the face opens during the stroke regardless of your mechanics. You've been blaming yourself for a design flaw.'
to_disrupt.copy_hook: You don't need a new stroke. You need a putter that doesn't fight the one you have.

to_disrupt.belief: Zero-torque putters like LAB Golf are the only way to get true face stability
to_disrupt.counter_evidence: LAB Golf pioneered the face-stability conversation but their specific implementation (lie angle balance / zero torque) creates tradeoffs: unconventional aesthetics (Q0406), center-shaft requirement (Q0398), lightweight feel (Q0400), distance control issues (Q0326), aluminum face feel (Q0397). Forward CG weighting achieves face stability through a different mechanism without these tradeoffs. Zero torque is ONE solution. It is not the only solution.
to_disrupt.disruption_angle: Acknowledge LAB's innovation, then transcend it. 'LAB Golf proved that face stability matters. They were right about the problem. But zero torque is not the only way to solve it -- and it comes with tradeoffs that don't work for every golfer. Forward weighting is the next evolution: face stability without the compromises.'
to_disrupt.copy_hook: LAB Golf was right about the problem. Face stability matters. But zero torque is just one solution -- and it comes with tradeoffs. Forward weighting is the next evolution. Same result. No compromises.

to_disrupt.belief: A $399 putter from Performance Golf can't compete with established brands like Scotty Cameron, LAB, or TaylorMade
to_disrupt.counter_evidence: Brand heritage does not determine physics. The RS1's forward CG weighting is an engineering innovation independent of brand lineage. At $399, it is priced below LAB Golf ($449-549) and at parity with Scotty Cameron -- while delivering a measurably different CG position. Q0160: 'While your clubs aren't generally your problem, I would say, from recent personal experience, that the putter may be the exception to that rule.' Engineering credibility comes from the mechanism, not the logo.
to_disrupt.disruption_angle: Position RS1 as the engineer's choice, not the brand loyalist's choice. 'You can pay for a logo or you can pay for physics. The RS1 is the only putter in its price range that puts the center of gravity forward of the shaft. That's not marketing. That's a measurement you can verify.'
to_disrupt.copy_hook: Not marketing. Engineering. Here's the proof.

to_align.belief: Face angle at impact is the most important variable in putting accuracy -- it determines 80%+ of directional error
to_align.validation_messaging: This belief is already held by the most educated segment of the WEB and is spreading via YouTube, GolfWRX, and LAB Golf's marketing. RS1 does not need to create this belief -- it needs to amplify and validate it, then present Forward Weighting as the best solution to the face-angle problem.
to_align.alignment_angle: Validate their growing awareness with data: 'You already know face angle matters. The research confirms it: face angle determines 80% or more of your putt's starting direction. The question is not whether face angle matters -- it's what your putter does about it.'
to_align.copy_hook: You already suspected it. Now the data proves it. Face angle at impact determines 80% of where your putt starts. Every other variable is noise.

to_align.belief: Putting is the fastest path to lower scores -- it's the highest-leverage area of the game to improve
to_align.validation_messaging: Golfers already know this. They track putts per round. They know their GIR-to-score gap. They know 'you drive for show and putt for dough.' Validate this awareness and make RS1 the logical next step: 'You've identified putting as the lever. Now pull it.'
to_align.alignment_angle: Mirror their analysis back to them: 'You hit 10 greens and shot 82. You know exactly where those strokes went. Putting is where you gain or lose 3-7 strokes every round. No other change you can make has this much impact this fast.'
to_align.copy_hook: 3-5 strokes. That's what's hiding between your ball-striking and your score. And it's all on the green.

to_align.belief: A putter that 'does the work for you' exists -- equipment can genuinely reduce the demands on the golfer's technique and mental game
to_align.validation_messaging: This belief is validated by LAB Golf converts and face-stable putter users who describe the experience as 'it almost putts by itself' (Q0444), 'gets the ball in the hole with little to no user intervention' (Q0422), 'less to think about' (Q0235). RS1 aligns with and amplifies this belief.
to_align.alignment_angle: Validate the hope with physics: 'You've heard golfers say their face-stable putter 'does the work.' It's not magic -- it's center of gravity placement. When the CG is forward of the shaft, the face falls square naturally. You don't steer. You let it fall.'
to_align.copy_hook: The putter that does the work for you is not a fantasy. It's physics. When the CG moves forward of the shaft, the face falls square on its own. You just let it happen.

to_align.belief: You should use the club you use most (putter) at least as much as the club you use least -- the per-use investment logic justifies premium putter pricing
to_align.validation_messaging: This rationalization already exists in the market and is frequently used by golfers justifying premium putter purchases. Amplify it: 'You use your putter 30-36 times per round. You use your driver 14 times. Why does your driver cost more than your putter?'
to_align.alignment_angle: Make the math undeniable: 'At $399, if you play 50 rounds per year, that's $7.98 per round. If the RS1 saves you 3 strokes per round, that's $2.66 per stroke saved. Compare that to the cost of a putting lesson, a round of golf, or the last putter you bought and discarded.'
to_align.copy_hook: You spend $600 on a driver you hit 14 times. Spend $399 on the club you use 32 times.

to_align.belief: Switching to a face-stable putter can produce immediate, measurable improvement -- not just a temporary honeymoon
to_align.validation_messaging: The strongest proof comes from user testimonials with specific metrics: Q0363 ('+2.3 strokes gained'), Q0358 ('dropped 10 strokes in 5 rounds'), Q0421 ('28 putts per round with NO 3 PUTTS'), Q0024 ('shaved 4 strokes off my game' on the first round). These are not honeymoon effects -- they are physics effects.
to_align.alignment_angle: Separate RS1 improvement from honeymoon effect: 'The honeymoon effect happens because confidence is temporarily boosted. RS1 improvement persists because the physics are permanent. The face stays square on day 1 and day 365. The CG doesn't move back.'
to_align.copy_hook: 4 strokes off his game. First round. No practice. Not confidence -- physics. The CG doesn't know it's day one.

to_align.belief: The putter industry has a hidden design flaw that nobody talks about -- and solving it requires a fundamentally different approach
to_align.validation_messaging: This belief is the RS1's master narrative. LAB Golf began this conversation; RS1 expands it. The hidden flaw: every conventional putter puts the CG behind the shaft, causing the face to drift open. This is not a manufacturing defect -- it is an industry-wide design paradigm that has never been questioned by the mainstream brands.
to_align.alignment_angle: Name the flaw. Make it undeniable. 'Every putter in every store -- Scotty Cameron, TaylorMade, Odyssey, Ping -- puts the center of gravity behind the shaft. That means the face wants to drift open during your stroke. You've been compensating for this your entire putting career without knowing it. The RS1 is the first premium mallet that puts the CG forward.'
to_align.copy_hook: Every putter you've ever owned has the same hidden flaw. The CG sits behind the shaft. The face drifts open. You compensate. You miss. The RS1 changes the physics.

synthesis.primary_want: Consistency that converts ball-striking quality into actual scores -- closing the 3-7 stroke gap between how well they hit the ball and what the scorecard says
synthesis.dominant_emotion: Frustration deepening into anxiety -- the maddening experience of doing the hard work (ball-striking) only to have it erased on the green, compounded by the self-reinforcing doom loop where missed putts destroy confidence which causes more missed putts
synthesis.key_belief_to_disrupt: Putting is mostly mental and equipment barely matters -- this belief prevents golfers from addressing the mechanical root cause (rearward CG causing face drift) and traps them in an endless cycle of technique changes, grip changes, and mental techniques that cannot solve a physics problem
synthesis.key_belief_to_align: Face angle at impact determines 80%+ of directional accuracy, and a putter engineered with forward CG weighting can mechanically keep the face square without requiring active compensation -- turning equipment from an obstacle into an ally
synthesis.master_narrative: The RS1 prospect is a serious amateur golfer -- typically a 5-15 handicap, age 35-55 -- who hits the ball well enough to be 3-5 strokes better than their scorecard shows. They have tried multiple putters, possibly had fittings, and have spent years blaming their technique for a problem that is actually caused by their equipment's physics. Every putter they have owned puts the center of gravity behind the shaft, causing the face to drift open during the stroke. They compensate by steering, gripping tighter, and overthinking -- which works on the practice green but fails under pressure when grip pressure rises and the face drifts more. This creates a doom loop: miss, lose confidence, grip tighter, drift more, miss again. They may be aware of LAB Golf's zero-torque solution but are blocked by its aesthetics, feel, price, or stroke-type requirements. The RS1 enters their world as the putter they did not know existed: face stability through forward CG weighting, premium mallet aesthetics, natural feel, compatibility with all stroke types, and a price point $50-150 below LAB. The transformation is not just mechanical -- it is emotional (from dread to calm), cognitive (from overthinking to quiet mind), behavioral (from steering to letting go), and identity-level (from 'I can't putt' to 'I'm a complete golfer'). The one thing that makes this transformation possible is moving the center of gravity forward of the shaft -- so the face falls square instead of drifting open. That is not marketing. That is physics.

Make the Putts You Should Be Making
Break Through Your Scoring Plateau -- From the Green

One Less Variable to Think About

What If Your Next Putter Actually Works?

emotional_arc_for_vsl: STAGE 1 -- RECOGNITION: Mirror their frustration. 'You hit greens. You three-putt. You practice. It doesn't transfer. You've tried 5 putters. Nothing sticks.' Make them feel seen.

STAGE 2 -- REVELATION: Name the hidden flaw. 'Every putter you've ever owned puts the CG behind the shaft. The face drifts open during your stroke. You compensate by steering. Under pressure, you over-compensate. That's the miss.'

STAGE 3 -- MECHANISM: Introduce forward CG weighting. 'What if the CG was in front of the shaft? The face would fall square on its own. No steering. No compensating. No fighting. That's what the RS1 does.'
STAGE 4 -- PROOF: Show it. 'Let it fall. Watch where the face points. That's not zero torque -- it's forward weighting. Different physics. Same result. Better feel.'

STAGE 5 -- TRANSFORMATION: Promise the desired state. 'Imagine standing over a 4-footer and feeling nothing but quiet certainty. That's what face stability feels like. Not confidence tricks. Physics.'
STAGE 6 -- RESOLUTION: Close with identity. 'You're not a bad putter. You've been putting with bad physics. The RS1 changes the physics. Your score finally reflects what your ball-striking deserves.'
belief_disruption_sequence: FIRST: Disrupt 'it's all mental' with face angle data -- establish that equipment matters mechanically
SECOND: Disrupt 'all putters are the same' with CG position comparison -- show the hidden design flaw
THIRD: Disrupt 'practice more' with practice-to-course transfer data -- explain why it fails under pressure
FOURTH: Disrupt 'LAB is the only answer' with forward weighting vs zero torque comparison -- present the third way
FIFTH: Align 'face angle is everything' with RS1 mechanism -- make RS1 the logical conclusion of their own growing awareness
